The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai City|ja|加西市|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|12=draft|12=draft}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|ja|北条政子|12=draft|12=draft}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|ja|本殿|12=draft|12=draft}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=draft|12=draft}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=draft|12=draft}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|ja|永禄|12=draft|12=draft}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=draft|12=draft}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=draft|12=draft}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District|ja|加東郡|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyogo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|ja|北条政子|12=draft|12=draft}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|ja|本殿|12=draft|12=draft}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=draft|12=draft}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=draft|12=draft}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|ja|永禄|12=draft|12=draft}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft|12=draft}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>

Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=draft|12=draft}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location={{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34|54|58|N|134|57|53|E|scale:30000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0357.jpg|280px|Saho Shrine main building]] |Map={{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former {{ill|Yashiro|ja|社町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai City|ja|加西市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|ja|北条政子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|ja|本殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|ja|永禄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District|ja|加東郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyogo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location={{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34|54|58|N|134|57|53|E|scale:30000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0357.jpg|280px|Saho Shrine main building]] |Map={{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyogo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former {{ill|Yashiro|ja|社町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai City|ja|加西市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|ja|北条政子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|ja|本殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|ja|永禄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District|ja|加東郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyogo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District|ja|加東郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Hyogo Prefecture" to "Hyōgo Prefecture"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location={{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34|54|58|N|134|57|53|E|scale:30000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0357.jpg|280px|Saho Shrine main building]] |Map={{location map|Japan Hyogo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former {{ill|Yashiro|ja|社町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai City|ja|加西市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|ja|北条政子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|ja|本殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|ja|永禄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District|ja|加東郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyogo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location={{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō City|qq=}}, Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34|54|58|N|134|57|53|E|scale:30000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|ja|天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|qq=}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0357.jpg|280px|Saho Shrine main building]] |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Katō City|ja|加東市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō City|qq=}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo Prefecture|qq=}}.<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former {{ill|Yashiro|ja|社町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro|qq=}} developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai City|ja|加西市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasai City|qq=}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|qq=}}, a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai Sect|qq=}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|ja|北条政子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōjō Masako|qq=}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|ja|本殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honden|qq=}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torii|qq=}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|qq=}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|ja|永禄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku|qq=}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkyō|qq=}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District|ja|加東郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō District|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai City|ja|加西市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasai City|qq=}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|qq=}}, a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai Sect|qq=}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō|qq=}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|ja|北条政子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōjō Masako|qq=}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|ja|本殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honden|qq=}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torii|qq=}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|qq=}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|ja|永禄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku|qq=}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enkyō|qq=}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District|ja|加東郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō District|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34|54|58|N|134|57|53|E|scale:30000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity=[[:en:Ame-no-Koyane|Ame-no-Koyane]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0357.jpg|280px|Saho Shrine main building]] |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|qq=}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|qq=}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34|54|58|N|134|57|53|E|scale:30000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity=[[:en:Ame-no-Koyane|Ame-no-Koyane]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0357.jpg|280px|Saho Shrine main building]] |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|qq=}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|qq=}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34|54|58|N|134|57|53|E|scale:30000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity=[[:en:Ame-no-Koyane|Ame-no-Koyane]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0357.jpg|280px|Saho Shrine main building]] |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|qq=}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|qq=}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11382936]] Wikidata name: Saho-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|qq=|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|qq=|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11382936]] Wikidata name: Saho-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]
