Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11382936]] Wikidata name: Saho-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11382936]] Wikidata name: Saho-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11382936]] Wikidata name: Saho-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11382936]] Wikidata name: Saho-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location=[[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Katō, Hyōgo|Katō City]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]].<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former [[:en:Yashiro, Hyōgo|Yashiro]] developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day [[:en:Kasai, Hyōgo|Kasai City]]<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Sect]] temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in [[:en:722|722]] ([[:en:Yōrō|Yōrō]] 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the shrine was venerated by [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]], who funded the rebuilding of the [[:en:Honden|Honden]] and established eight wooden [[:en:Torii|Torii]] at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in [[:en:Eiroku|Eiroku]] 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to [[:en:Enkyō (Edo period)|Enkyō]] 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former [[:en:Katō District, Hyōgo|Katō District]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō (Edo period)|en|Enkyō (Edo period)|WD=Q1183728|lt=Enkyō}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District, Hyōgo|en|Katō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1933120|lt=Katō District}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11382936]] Wikidata name: Saho-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location={{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō City}}, Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō City}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}.<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former {{ill|Yashiro, Hyōgo|en|Yashiro, Hyōgo|WD=Q2189377|lt=Yashiro}} developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai, Hyōgo|en|Kasai, Hyōgo|WD=Q851155|lt=Kasai City}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Sect}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in {{ill|722|en|722|WD=Q23542|lt=722}} ({{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Honden}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=Torii}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō (Edo period)|en|Enkyō (Edo period)|WD=Q1183728|lt=Enkyō}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District, Hyōgo|en|Katō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1933120|lt=Katō District}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saho Shrine]][[ja:佐保神社]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Saho Shrine |location={{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō City}}, Yashiro 777 |coordinates={{Coord|34.916111|N|134.964722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity={{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}} |Rank=Prefectural Shrine |Honden= |Foundation=23rd year of Emperor Suinin's reign (traditionally 76 BCE) |Reisai=October 16 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyogo 佐保神社社殿（兵庫県加東市社）例祭 DSCF0357.jpg |Map={{location map|Japan Hyōgo|lat_deg=34|lat_min=54|lat_sec=58|lon_deg=134|lon_min=57|lon_sec=53|label=Saho Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center}} |prefecture={{ill|Kato|WD=Q877164}} }}
'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō City}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}.<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former {{ill|Yashiro, Hyōgo|en|Yashiro, Hyōgo|WD=Q2189377|lt=Yashiro}} developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai, Hyōgo|en|Kasai, Hyōgo|WD=Q851155|lt=Kasai City}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Sect}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in {{ill|722|en|722|WD=Q23542|lt=722}} ({{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Honden}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=Torii}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō (Edo period)|en|Enkyō (Edo period)|WD=Q1183728|lt=Enkyō}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District, Hyōgo|en|Katō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1933120|lt=Katō District}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]

'''Saho Shrine''' (佐保神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Katō, Hyōgo|en|Katō, Hyōgo|WD=Q877164|lt=Katō City}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}.<ref name="jinjacho">{{Cite web|url=http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html|title=Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> Historically known as Sakaai Shrine, it is recognized as one of the most significant shrines in the North Harima region, with the former {{ill|Yashiro, Hyōgo|en|Yashiro, Hyōgo|WD=Q2189377|lt=Yashiro}} developing as a {{ill|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}.<ref name="kato">{{Cite web|url=http://www.kato-kanko.jp/2017/03/soho-akimatsuri/|title=Katō City Tourism Association - Saho Shrine 'Autumn Festival'|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref>
==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai, Hyōgo|en|Kasai, Hyōgo|WD=Q851155|lt=Kasai City}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Sect}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in {{ill|722|en|722|WD=Q23542|lt=722}} ({{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Honden}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=Torii}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō (Edo period)|en|Enkyō (Edo period)|WD=Q1183728|lt=Enkyō}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District, Hyōgo|en|Katō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1933120|lt=Katō District}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>

==Overview==
The shrine is believed to have been founded in [[:en:76 BCE|76 BCE]] (23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}'s reign). Originally, it was located at Kamakura Peak in present-day {{ill|Kasai, Hyōgo|en|Kasai, Hyōgo|WD=Q851155|lt=Kasai City}}<ref>Modern site of {{ill|Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja|蓬莱山普光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraisan Fukō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Sect}} temple.</ref>, and relocated to its current location in {{ill|722|en|722|WD=Q23542|lt=722}} ({{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|WD=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō}} 6) following a divine message received by an elderly man named Abe no Saburōdaifu.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}, who funded the rebuilding of the {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Honden}} and established eight wooden {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=Torii}} at the inner and outer precincts.<ref name="jinjacho"/> While the original torii no longer exist, one western torii was replaced with a stone structure, still present in the Torii District of Yashiro.<ref name="jinjacho"/>
The shrine's precincts were devastated during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}} due to warfare. In {{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 16 (1547), a fire destroyed the {{ill|Shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} entirely. However, through the efforts of itinerant monks, the shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|WD=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku}} 7 (1564), though it never regained its former grandeur.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, successive shoguns granted the shrine a seal and a stipend of ten koku. In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 14 (1881), it was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.<ref name="jinjacho"/> The current structure dates to {{ill|Enkyō (Edo period)|en|Enkyō (Edo period)|WD=Q1183728|lt=Enkyō}} 4 (1747).<ref name="jinjacho"/>
=== Wakayanozan Jigen-ji ===
Before the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine was affiliated with {{ill|Kogi Shingon Sect|ja|古義真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogi Shingon Sect|1=Kogi Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134368473}} temple {{ill|Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja|涌羅野山慈眼寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayanozan Jigen-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2548820|title=Kogi Shingon Sect Temple List|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> The precincts included Buddhist facilities such as a bell tower, Goma Hall, and Yakushi Hall.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
Jigen-ji's creation predates the foundation of its {{ill|Tatchū|ja|塔頭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatchū|1=tatchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9769742}} temples, including Jiho-in (founded in the Hakuchi era).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref> Following the Shinbutsu Bunri decree, Jigen-ji was abolished, leaving Jiho-in and Zenryu-in.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==Festivals== * '''April 16''': Ritual bathing ceremony.<ref name="jinjacho"/><ref name="yaokami">{{Cite web|url=https://yaokami.jp/1283170/|title=Yaokami - Saho Shrine|accessdate=December 28, 2019}}</ref> * '''Day before Sports Day (October)''': Autumn Festival.<ref name="kato"/> Features vibrant floats and lion dances. * '''October 16''': Grand Autumn Festival.<ref>[http://www.senhime.com/hp/sahojinja/ Saho Shrine]</ref>
<gallery widths="180" heights="120"> File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival DSCF0325.jpg|Autumn Festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 灯籠 DSCF0330.jpg|Stone lanterns and festival grounds (October 9, 2011) File:Saho-Jinja Kato-Hyōgo 佐保神社（兵庫県加東市社）festival 百度石 DSCF0322.jpg|[[Noh Stage]] and [[Hyakudo Stone]] </gallery>
==History== Saho Shrine was the sole prefectural shrine in the former {{ill|Katō District, Hyōgo|en|Katō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1933120|lt=Katō District}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978702|title=Katō District Gazetteer|accessdate=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External Links== * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6311091.html Saho Shrine - Hyōgo Shrine Association] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SitPgbrRAc Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - 2013 YouTube Video] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a-AOnx1TkeE Saho Shrine Autumn Festival - Lion Dance Performance]
